New Claude Code Hijacks Mouse Clicks in Your Terminal? Kill It with CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS

Claude Code now captures mouse events inside the terminal to give you click-to-expand, hover, scroll acceleration, and so on — but that also means you can't select text or click links with the mouse anymore. To turn it off, there's
no settings.json field and no CLI flag
— only environment variables. The one I reach for the most is
(v2.1.195+), which kills clicks / drag / hover but keeps the scroll wheel.

After the recent Claude Code update, the terminal now supports clicking things inside it — Yes, No, that kind of stuff. The problem is, as a developer I usually have multiple projects open, and every time I switch back to a project I end up clicking Yes by accident — when I really didn't want to run the thing!
The whole experience is pretty rough, so I figured I'd document how to fix it.
Why is my mouse suddenly getting hijacked?
Basically, in v2.1.195 Claude Code added mouse interactions inside the terminal — drag, click, hover, all of it. Previous versions didn't have this.

The feature itself is fine I think, but the thing is, as a developer I'm constantly bouncing between N projects, and I live inside the VS Code integrated terminal. So every time I switch back to a project, my mouse click lands on
by accident and runs something I didn't want to run. Pretty bad experience.
And imagine Claude Code asks me whether to run:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
, and now stuff is gone that I didn't want gone. That's actually kind of serious.
The behavior is controlled by the environment variable
. This is the "kill click / drag / hover, keep the scroll wheel" version — for me personally this is enough.
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1 # Disable mouse click/drag/hover, keep scrolling
If you don't even want the scroll wheel hijacked, use the other one instead:
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=1 # Disable mouse capture entirely (scrolling included)
menu or settings.json field for this, so environment variables are the only way.
I'm on zsh so I'll only walk through zsh here, but if you're on bash or fish it's basically the same idea.
and add this at the bottom:

# Disable Claude Code mouse click/drag capture (keep scrolling)
export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1
# Disable Claude Code mouse click/drag capture (keep scrolling)

# Open a new terminal window
# Or run in your current window:
source ~/.zshrc
# Or run in your current window:
session won't pick this up automatically
— you have to quit and restart Claude Code for the new env var to take effect.

and you just need to select some text occasionally, every terminal has a
bypass key for the mouse capture:
VS Code also has a setting
directly force the cursor position — that's a VS Code-specific setting, nothing to do with Claude Code itself.
Adding mouse interaction to Claude Code is a nice idea in theory, but for people who constantly switch projects and like selecting text with the mouse, it's honestly pretty annoying.
The simplest fix is to drop
. Scroll wheel stays, clicks and drags stop getting hijacked, and you stop accidentally hitting YES and firing off commands you didn't want.

When you're working in Claude Code and try to select text in the terminal or click a link in an error message, the clicks get hijacked by Claude Code itself. This post walks through the difference between CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE and CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS, how to add it to your .zshrc, and the one-off bypass hotkeys for iTerm2, macOS Terminal, and VS Code when you don't want to touch your config.
